title: Holubtsi
subtitle: Ukrainian stuffed cabbage rolls in tomato sauce
excerpt: Cabbage leaves stuffed with pork, beef, and rice, braised in a tomato sauce until the leaves are silky and the filling is fully cooked through. The Ukrainian version of stuffed cabbage — larger rolls, dual-meat filling, and a brighter tomato sauce than the Hungarian or Polish equivalents.
sourceNotes: Holubtsi (from the Ukrainian word for doves) are the Ukrainian counterpart to Polish gołąbki and Hungarian töltött káposzta. All three dishes use the same core principle (meat and grain wrapped in cabbage leaves) but differ in filling composition and sauce. Ukrainian holubtsi typically use a mixture of pork and beef mince, sunflower rather than butter or lard, and are braised in a tomato-dominant sauce. The dish is eaten at Christmas Eve and throughout the year as an everyday dinner.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in the sauce. Defrost overnight and reheat covered at 170°C for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make in bulk and freeze in portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 days ahead. The flavour improves overnight in the fridge.

content.text: Holubtsi differ from Polish gołąbki in two principal ways: the filling uses both pork and beef mince (which gives a slightly more complex flavour than all-pork), and sunflower oil replaces butter as the fat in the filling and sauce. The tomato sauce is made from tinned tomatoes rather than a lighter stock-and-puree mixture, resulting in a more intensely flavoured braise.

The rice goes into the filling raw, as with all stuffed cabbage recipes of this type. It swells as it absorbs the braising liquid and meat juices, which is what gives the filling its cohesion. Ensure the sauce is deep enough in the dish to keep the rolls moist throughout the full cooking time.

Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Cut a deep X into the base of the {{cabbage-white}} and lower in. Peel off softened outer leaves as they loosen, 12 to 14 leaves total. Trim the thick central ribs. Mix the {{pork-mince}}, {{beef-mince}}, {{long-grain-rice}}, grated {{onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{sunflower-oil}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling near the base of each leaf, fold the sides in, and roll firmly.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Cook the chopped {{onion}} in {{sunflower-oil}} for 6 minutes in a saucepan. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the blended {{tinned-tomatoes}} and {{bay-leaves}} and season with {{sea-salt-fine}}. Simmer for 5 minutes. Line the base of a casserole dish with leftover cabbage leaves. Arrange the rolls seam-side down. Pour the sauce over to cover. Cover tightly with a lid or foil and bake for 80 to 90 minutes until the rice is cooked through. Serve with {{soured-cream}} at the table.

items.symptom: Rice hard in the centre of the rolls after 90 minutes
items.cause: Too little sauce or rolls packed too tightly
items.fix: Add 200 ml of hot water to the sauce, re-cover, and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes

items.symptom: Sauce scorched on the bottom of the dish
items.cause: Not enough sauce, or oven too hot
items.fix: Check after 45 minutes and add water if the sauce has reduced significantly. The rolls should remain fully submerged in sauce throughout

items.cause: Filling too loose or insufficient rice to bind
items.fix: Measure the rice carefully — it expands significantly and is what holds the filling together. Pack each roll tightly with no gaps

content.text: Holubtsi appear at almost every significant meal in Ukrainian domestic cooking: at Christmas Eve (Svyata Vechera), at Easter, at weddings, and at weekday dinners when something substantial and slow-cooked is wanted. They are made in large batches, often by the whole family working together, and improve over successive days as the tomato sauce reduces and the filling softens further. The dish crosses borders easily (nearly identical preparations exist in Polish, Hungarian, Romanian, and Jewish Eastern European cooking), but the Ukrainian version, with its dual-meat filling and sunflower-oil base, is distinct in flavour.
